user_story: As a Zoning Staff member, I want to Process the Requests for Zoning Information, so that I can capture my response to the request in the system and also send a communication from the system to the Applicant.
plantuml:
```plantuml
@startuml
participant ZoningStaff as "ZS"
participant System as "Sys"
participant Applicant as "App"

note right of Sys: Process Request for Zoning Information\nCapture response in system\nSend communication to applicant

ZS->>Sys: Submit request for zoning information
Sys-->>ZS: Validate request
alt Valid Request
    Sys->>ZS: Send form to complete response
    ZS->>Sys: Complete response and submit
    Sys->>Applicant: Send notification of completed response
else Invalid Request
    Sys->>ZS: Display error message
end

note right of App: Receive communication from system\nView response in the system
@enduml
```

user_story: As an API User, I want to be able to use data to get results from multiple datasets, So that I can build user experiences based on more than one dataset more easily.

```plantuml
activate data_service

data_service->>+database2: Retrieve data from database 2

database1-->>data_service: Return data from database 1
database2-->>data_service: Return data from database 2

data_service->>user: Return combined results
deactivate data_service

@enduml
```

This sequence diagram shows the flow of interactions between an API user and a data service, where the data service retrieves data from multiple databases to return combined results.

user_story: As an API User, I want to be able to use data to get results from multiple datasets, So that I can build user experiences based on more than one dataset more easily.

```plantuml
participant DataAPI as "Data Service"

title Multi-Dataset Query Sequence Diagram

APIUser->>DataAPI: Get data from multiple datasets
DataAPI->>Database1: Retrieve data from Dataset 1
alt Dataset 2 available
    DataAPI->>Database2: Retrieve data from Dataset 2
else
    DataAPI->>Database1: Retrieve all data (no other dataset found)
end

DataAPI->>APIUser: Return combined results
@enduml
```
This sequence diagram illustrates how the API user can retrieve and combine data from multiple datasets. The `alt` block shows an alternative flow for when a secondary dataset is available, otherwise it falls back to retrieving all data from the primary dataset.

user_story: As a cook, I want to have employee data on the system including clock in and out information.
plantuml:
```plantuml
@startuml
participant Cook
participant ClockInSystem
participant Database

Cook->>ClockInSystem: Get Employee Data
alt Employee exists in database
  ClockInSystem-->Database: Get employee data from database
  alt Employee clocked out before
    Database-->ClockInSystem: Return clock-out status
    ClockInSystem-->>Cook: Display clock-in information to Cook
  else
    ClockInSystem-->Database: Update clock-in status in database
    Database-->ClockInSystem: Save new clock-in data
    ClockInSystem-->>Cook: Display clock-in confirmation message to Cook
  end
else
  ClockInSystem-->>Cook: Display employee not found error to Cook
endif

alt Employee wants to clock out
  Cook->>ClockInSystem: Get Clock-Out Button
  alt Employee is still on clocked in status
    ClockInSystem-->Database: Update clock-out status in database
    Database-->ClockInSystem: Save new clock-out data
    ClockInSystem-->>Cook: Display clock-out confirmation message to Cook
  else
    ClockInSystem-->>Cook: Display already clocked out error to Cook
  end

end
@enduml
```

This sequence diagram illustrates the interactions between the cook, the clock-in system, and the database when an employee wants to clock in or out. The diagram covers two main use cases:

1.  **Clocking In:** When a cook tries to get employee data from the clock-in system.
2.  **Clocking Out:** When an employee wants to clock out.

The PlantUML code is written in a way that it's easy to follow and understand, with clear labels and arrows illustrating the flow of interactions between participants.

user_story: As a cook, I want to have employee data on the system including clock in and out information.

```plantuml
S->>C: Employee data loaded (e.g., John Smith)
S->>C: Time recorded (e.g., 08:00)

C->S: Request employee data update
S->>C: Update employee data (e.g., updated location, shift change)

deactivate S

@endsequence
```

```plantuml
alt Event record is invalid
  EventStore-->Archivist: Return error message
else
  EventStore->>Archivist: Record created successfully
end

activate Archivist

Archivist->>EventStore: Edit Event record
```

This sequence diagram illustrates how an Archivist can create and edit Event records in the Event Store. The valid cases show the successful creation or editing of a record, while the alternative case shows what happens when there's an invalid event record. 

The `alt` keyword is used to indicate that one of the branches will be taken but not both at the same time. In this scenario, it signifies an 'else' condition and represents any other valid situation other than creating or editing a record with invalid data.

Note: This PlantUML code snippet assumes a basic understanding of PlantUML syntax. If you're new to PlantUML, I recommend checking their official documentation for more details on how to create sequence diagrams.
